name: Ixion
description: Ixion, in Greek mythology, stands as a cautionary figure, a king of the Lapiths in Thessaly condemned to eternal torment for his hubristic actions. His story serves as a stark reminder of the consequences of violating the sacred laws of hospitality and disrespecting the divine order. Ixion murdered his father-in-law, Eioneus, by trapping him in a pit of burning coals, an act that rendered him an outcast among both mortals and gods. Zeus, pitying his plight, invited Ixion to Olympus, but instead of gratitude, Ixion lusted after Hera, Zeus's wife. This ultimate act of treachery prompted Zeus to create a cloud in Hera's likeness, Nephele, whom Ixion coupled with, resulting in the birth of Centaurus, the progenitor of the centaurs. As punishment for his audacious behavior, Zeus had Hermes bind Ixion to a winged, eternally spinning wheel of fire in Tartarus, a symbol of unending suffering and a testament to the dangers of unchecked ambition and impiety. Ixion's fate serves as a powerful moral lesson within the broader context of Greek myth, highlighting the importance of respect, piety, and the avoidance of hubris.
subtitle: King of the Lapiths

keyMyths.title: Ixion's Murder of Eioneus
keyMyths.description: Ixion, king of the Lapiths, sought to marry Dia, the daughter of Eioneus. Eioneus demanded a substantial bride-price, which Ixion refused to pay. Instead, he invited Eioneus to his palace under the guise of reconciliation, only to trap him in a pit filled with burning coals, resulting in a gruesome death. This act of murder violated the sacred laws of hospitality, rendering Ixion an outcast, shunned by both gods and men. No one was willing to purify him of his crime until Zeus intervened.

keyMyths.title: Ixion's Attempt on Hera
keyMyths.description: Zeus, taking pity on Ixion, invited him to Olympus. However, Ixion repaid this kindness with lustful intentions towards Hera, Zeus's wife. Zeus, aware of Ixion's desires, created a cloud in Hera's likeness, named Nephele, and placed it in Ixion's path. Ixion, deceived, coupled with the cloud, believing it to be Hera. From this union was born Centaurus, who would later mate with mares, fathering the race of centaurs, creatures known for their wild and untamed nature.

keyMyths.title: Ixion's Punishment in Tartarus
keyMyths.description: Upon discovering Ixion's treachery and attempted seduction of Hera (or, more accurately, Nephele), Zeus was enraged. He ordered Hermes to bind Ixion to a winged, eternally spinning wheel of fire. This wheel was then cast into Tartarus, the deepest and darkest region of the Underworld, where Ixion would endure unending torment. His punishment serves as a perpetual reminder of the consequences of hubris and disrespect towards the gods, a fate worse than death itself.

sources.author: Pseudo-Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology, providing a systematic account of various myths and legends, including the story of Ixion.

sources.author: Hyginus

sources.author: Pindar
